summary: DHH announces a pivot for the ONCE brand: after the original paid self-hosted web app model failed to gain traction beyond Campfire, 37signals released those apps as free open source software, which succeeded wildly. Now they're doubling down by building a new application server — also called ONCE — that makes self-hosting a full suite of apps dead simple from a single machine. The new ONCE provides a terminal UI for metrics, zero-downtime upgrades, and scheduled backups. The pitch is consolidation: one box, one command, all your apps.
key_points: The original paid self-hosting model (one-time fee per app) failed commercially — only Campfire recouped its investment
Pivoting to free open source (Campfire, Writebook, Fizzy) worked: broad adoption, community contributions, and real production learning
The new ONCE is an application server that consolidates multiple self-hosted apps onto a single machine, eliminating per-app dedicated boxes
It ships with a terminal UI covering metrics (RAM, CPU, traffic), zero-downtime upgrades, and scheduled backups
37signals is positioning ONCE as infrastructure for both their own apps and AI-agent-built applications
money_quotes: You gotta listen when the market tells you what it wants!
Now we're doubling down on the gift.
Installing a whole suite of applications on your own server should be dead easy.
The original ONCE model wanted a dedicated box or VM per app, which was just cumbersome and costly to maintain.
Now you can use a single machine — even your laptop! — to run everything all at once.
It's meant to be able to run all the infrastructure apps you'd need, like our full suite and all the ones your AI agents will soon be building for you.
tone: pragmatic and enthusiastic — candid about past failure, upbeat about the new direction
key_insight: When paid self-hosting failed, going fully open source unlocked real adoption, and now 37signals is building the server infrastructure to make self-hosting genuinely frictionless.
